data.title: Kura Sushi: Fire In Gasoline As Tariff Pause Sparks A Rally
data.description:
Fire in Gasoline

Funny how things turn around.

Kura Sushi (KRUS) had been sliding with the market after the whole ‘Liberation Day’ news and some pretty bleak sentiment. But all it took was a 90-day pause on tariffs and a smoother-than-expected transition quarter and... boom, Kura Sushi stock shot up nearly 30% on Wednesday.

Well, who am I to complain? I had just upgraded the stock to a ‘Buy’ with a $90 price target after watching this growth story lose nearly half its market cap since the start of the year.

It was time to bite this temaki near the support zone! After all, share prices behave very cyclically, and it was obvious that it was time to go shopping.

But of course, things had to get a little messy first.

The White House came out swinging with tariffs on the so-called ‘worst offenders,’ and whatever was left of market optimism pretty much vanished. Inflation expectations jumped, growth expectations dropped, and the market — already bleeding since February — took another hit as the full scope of the measures became clear.

For restaurants, the signal was loud and clear: tighter wallets ahead. Fewer visits, lower average checks, guests were about to get even more selective with their spending.

So who comes out on top in a setup like this?

Easy answer. The spots that give you the most bang for your buck, the famous 'value-for-dollar'. Think steakhouses like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) and LongHorn from Darden (DRI), both masters at delivering tremendous value.

Even Cracker Barrel (CBRL), with its early-bird specials — basically Texas Roadhouse for the family dining crowd — should keep riding the wave, especially now that the turnaround is starting to stick.

The biggest losers?

In my opinion, concepts like fad fast-casuals with high-price points could lose steam. Of course, fast-food restaurants where you wouldn't get a good value-for-dollar ratio would be decimated again, following the cycle of declining value scores.

Let’s be real, Kura Sushi is a fun and good spot for Japanese food. But it’s not exactly your go-to for everyday value. Price points run a bit higher than what you’d find at your typical casual dining place. It’s almost the same as GEN Korean BBQ (GENK).

A cut in discretionary spending would definitely hurt this growth story. The CEO himself said he saw a tariff-related slowdown in April. It’s probably the wealth effect kicking in. After all, we just saw a sharp market drop and a wave of selling in retirement funds.

But that’s not all.

Don’t believe me? Let’s look at some numbers.

First off, the Trump Tariffs would tack on an extra $300,000 to $400,000 in construction costs per store. That pushes the total cost of opening a new Kura location to somewhere between $2.8 and $2.9 million, a pretty steep jump of 12% to 16% in buildout costs! Consider that they plan to open 14 locations during FY 2025. Assuming stable construction costs throughout the year, they would add at least $4.2 to 5.6 million in full-year Capex to build out the same stores.

But if you zoom in a little more, the hit gets even clearer.

We’re talking about an extra year just to reach the payback period — plus a 10% to 14% drop in the cost-benefit ratio. That’s a big deal for a growth story built on opening new units efficiently.

However, those are still solid numbers compared to other restaurant chains.

Take Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), for example. Their restaurants are massive, and while they pull in nearly $8 million in AUV, the buildout costs average around $4.5 million. That puts their payback period in the 4-to-5-year range, with a cost-benefit ratio that’s about 12% lower than Kura Sushi, even in the worst-case scenario under the tariffs.

Another example is GEN Korean BBQ. They also have a three-year payback period, but demonstrate a lower Cost-Benefit Ratio of 7.74%. And even the heavyweight Texas Roadhouse isn’t immune to this. With a massive $8.5 million per unit cost, they’re looking at a six-year payback and a Cost-Benefit Ratio of just 2.87%.

So yeah, while it wasn’t exactly a blowout, Kura Sushi’s Q2 FY2025 still did its job.

It reaffirmed the growth story.

They held steady on their full-year outlook, including double-digit unit growth and a 20% unit-level margin. Now they just need to get sales momentum back and start spreading those fixed costs around.

But let’s be real — it wasn’t just this reaffirmation that got the stock moving today.

The tariff pause changes a few things right off the bat:

  1. More time to make reciprocal agreements;
  2. It reduces the chances of an economic slowdown in the coming months. Doesn’t take it off the table completely, but it definitely eases the pressure;
  3. Perhaps some dissenting voices within the Trump cycle may be acting to dissuade this policy.

This likely means we get at least one more quarter of somewhat normal consumer spending and stable inflation. That’s great news for Kura! It gives them more breathing room to work things out with suppliers (who already said they’ll take on much of the tariff hit), fine-tune the marketing plan, and keep pushing the development pipeline forward.

The combination of these two factors was like fire on gasoline. The stock was already going up slightly today after the release of Q2 FY 2025 results. But the tariff pause was almost the best of both worlds.

A wave of capital came rushing back into the stock market, and suddenly, names that had been trading well below their peer group and historical averages started catching some well-deserved attention.

Same-Store Sales Drop Scares, But Doesn't Bite

I grew up watching cartoons, and honestly, watching what Kura Sushi went through earlier this year felt like one of those classic beatdowns. To get into the oriental atmosphere, not even Goku would come out of this unscathed.

Blizzards, California wildfires, tariffs, IP programming gaps, cannibalization — you name it. Phew! I’m getting exhausted just listing it all.

By the end of the day, sales continued to climb. They were $64.9 million—up at least 13.2% year-over-year. This was driven by two factors:

  1. Double-digit growth in the number of restaurants year-over-year; and
  2. Outperforming units that have recently opened (Bakersfield, CA is a great example of this).

This double-digit revenue growth for the quarter was partially offset by weaker same-store sales.

They fell 5.3% year-over-year in Q2 FY 2024. This represented a 710 bp slowdown from the previous quarter. Breaking that down a bit, we have an 8.5% year-over-year decline in traffic—accelerating the sequential decline from FY 2024—offset by average check per guest growth of 3.2% year-over-year.

But wait, let me explain why this isn’t as bad as you might think.

First off, as I mentioned, Kura Sushi is expanding its footprint at a seriously fast pace — and I mean very fast. Stores like the ones in Bakersfield or even the ones in the Northwest aren't included in the comparable stores count.

That means their stronger-than-expected performances can only be glimpsed through revenue.

Another factor, as I mentioned earlier, was natural phenomena. Management estimates that weather alone created a drag of around 400 to 500 basis points! If there were no headwinds in this regard (I mean, literally) Kura Sushi would see same-store sales decline by only 1.3% year-over-year to 0.3% year-over-year depending on traffic impacts.

Just look. That wouldn’t have been too bad. Black Box Intelligence showed the industry was basically flat — up 2.5% year-over-year in January and down 2.5% in February, canceling each other out.

The third and final factor is: Management already knew Q2 FY 2025 would be the softest quarter of the year — thanks to the IP gap and some tough comps from last year’s hit 'Peanuts' campaign. I actually flagged this in my last piece on Kura Sushi — they fumbled the timing of their marketing campaign, and now they’re playing catch-up in the second half of FY 2025.

See? They look very angry, but they don't bite.

Now that we've got the dots on the i's and crosses on the t's, let's talk about something that hurt the P&L in Q2: increases in labor costs.

They grew from 180 bp to 34.8% due to the deleveraging of sales. The remedy for this is simple, but not always so quick: increase sales volume.

The deleveraging of sales also hurt SG&A, which also increased in importance relative to revenue. Additionally, Kura Sushi reported a litigation settlement expense of $2.1 million.

All of this was offset by lower Food, Beverage and Packaging costs of 90 bp to 28.7%.

Still, that wasn’t enough to stop the bleeding. EBIT came in negative at $4.6 million.

With a small tailwind from Investment Income, Kura Sushi reported a net loss of $3.8 million — that’s EPS of -$0.31, down $0.22 from the same period last year. Normalized and diluted EPS came in at -$0.19, a $0.14 drop year-over-year.

Not ideal, sure, but considering everything we’ve walked through above, it still lines up with a growth story that’s just hitting a few bumps along the way.

And hey, you’re not the only one who saw an opportunity in the bear market. Kura Sushi deployed $15.3 million into marketable and equity securities. Since these are highly liquid, the company still ended the quarter with $85.2 million in cash and zero long-term debt.

Not bad at all.

But let’s get to the heart of the thesis: pipeline expansion.

Kura’s Conveyor of Growth Keeps Moving

In Q2 FY 2024, Kura Sushi opened three new restaurants: Berkeley, CA; Fort Worth, TX; Paramus, NJ. For Q3 so far they have also opened two restaurants: Scottsdale, AZ and Lynnwood, WA.

It’s worth pointing out that these new openings still don’t fully reflect the ‘50-50 Strategy’ management introduced after identifying cannibalization risks.

Kura still has a heavy footprint in California (21 units), Texas (12), and New Jersey (5). These locations were probably greenlit a while ago.

Remember, the real shift was promised for next year—that’s when we’ll see whether the new game plan kicks in for real.

Things really started to get interesting last year. When they opened in places like Tacoma, WA; Beaverton, OR; and especially Bakersfield, CA, it became clear—they didn't need to chase those big, flashy DMAs to pull strong AUVs.

Even mid-to-low DMA areas were showing strong performance and sky-high AUVs.

In other words, if it weren't for Tacoma's success they would never have doubled down on Washington with Lynnwood!

That basically unlocked a whole new white space for Kura to grow into. That said, they’re now setting their sights on markets like Birmingham, Tulsa, Boise, and Oklahoma City. New additions in Boulder, CO and Salt Lake City, UT are coming soon.

Just to recap, Kura Sushi currently has 75 restaurants. They opened five this year alone and are on track to reach their guidance goal of 14 openings throughout FY 2025.

This brings the restaurant count to 84 locations and at least one more opening throughout FY 2025 (through August of this year).

Another double-digit year-over-year unit growth is practically guaranteed.

FY 2025 Guidance Reiterated

Further proof that a soft Q2 was expected? Look no further than management reaffirming full-year guidance during the last earnings call.

If you don't remember or didn't see it, it's as follows:

  • Total sales are between $275 million and $279 million (estimated growth of 16% year-over-year to 17% year-over-year);
  • Openings of 14 units;
  • Average net capital expenditures per unit of approximately $2.5 million ($35 million Capex for openings in the consolidated year);
  • G&A as a percentage of sales is approximately 13.5% (based on revenue guidance it would be $37.12 million to $37.6 million, growing roughly 10% year-over-year).

My Recommendation Is Still 'Buy'

Kura Sushi continues to be a delicious growth story—with a built-in margin of safety of roughly 40% based on a price target of $90.

And if you were sharp enough to buy during the last Trump Tariff dip at $43? You didn’t just lock in over 30% upside—you walked away with a juicy 52% margin of safety.

With an estimated RPS of around $27.43 (taking into account estimated revenue of $331.95 million for FY 2026), Kura Sushi is trading at a P/S of approximately 1.98x.

Sounds high, right?

But keep in mind, Kura trades at a 5-year average sales multiple of just over 4x.

That’s relatively modest when you compare it to other fast-growing restaurant names like Shake Shack (SHAK), Wingstop (WING), and CAVA (CAVA), which all command significantly higher premiums thanks to their growth profiles.

But we don’t need to dwell on estimates. On a historical basis, Kura Sushi still has much lower multiples than other high-growth restaurant chains:

Back when Kura Sushi was trading near resistance earlier this year, its P/S multiple was actually more stretched than CAVA’s. Since then, as growth cooled, the gap widened.

But here’s the thing—even with CAVA and Wingstop pulling back double digits year-to-date, they still look pricey on both P/E and EV/S metrics.

That’s not the case for my two favorite names in the high-growth restaurant space right now: Kura Sushi and Shake Shack, especially after their recent pullbacks.

That said, I’m reiterating my ‘Buy’ rating on Kura Sushi, with a price target of $90.

data.title: HTD: Don't Let Taxes Distract You From Compounding
data.description:
The John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD) was created for folks whose tax situation may require them to care about the tax treatment of their portfolio income. Yet, dividends are dividends, and this fund offers a steady yield among dependable securities known for regular distributions from essential business models. For that reason, I consider it a reasonable buy for the investor who seeks to navigate a potential bear market through dividend reinvestment.

Historical Results

The benefits of this fund show themselves when we look at the total returns of HTD over the last decade.

While the S&P 500 has come out ahead in the past decade, we see this mostly occurred in the last few years, on the heels of an AI-driven, tech-focus bulled run. During years when there is less excitement in the markets, however, HTD offered a clear advantage through dividend reinvestment and compounding.

This is in spite of the fact that dividends haven't grown much over time, being cyclical if anything. Nevertheless, they've been a steady source of return, even in times of stress, such as the days of the COVID pandemic. Despite the lack of an increasing dividend, it does go to show what is possible with simple reinvestment.

Composition and Strategy

In a mission to provide tax-advantaged dividends to shareholders, HTD primarily invests in U.S. common stocks that are known to be dividend payers.

For this reason, the fund is heavily concentrated on utilities and financials. It's worth noting, however, that not everything fits into this neat box of dividend stocks.

Above, a significant part of this sector allocation is realized through corporate bonds, not only common stocks. Some of these issues span into the next century, commanding a very high yield. If the income is attractive, it seems that HTD's manager doesn't let the potential tax treatment get in the way. As of their most recent statement of holdings, these bonds made up 31% of the portfolio. Some preferred stock also finds its way into the portfolio.

The fund also utilizes some leverage, often up to a third of their total assets. Their liquidity agreement with State Street Bank and Trust Company provides them a line of credit, securities lending, and reverse repurchase agreements to finance these investments. To hedge some of this interest rate risk on the financing, they also own some swap contracts. In the event of a market crash, they may have to liquidate some of their holdings as collateral to repay these loans.

With the interest expense from their financing making up most of the expenses in this fund, the management's cut is actually a fee of 1.13%.

In the grand scheme, this is a portfolio weighted on American utilities and financials. Mostly in stocks, it generally has some long-term growth opportunities, but it also pursues high-yielding bonds from some of these same issuers where possible. With a bit of leverage, this helps to juice up its yield.

Future Outlook

One of the good things about this fund is that its holdings weren't trading at huge multiples like many other stock funds, particularly those with tech exposure. While many were worried about a stock market bubble, a portfolio primarily of utilities and financials is largely an interest rate game. As rates increased starting in 2022, valuations were pushed down.

The share price started to recover in 2024, likely due to expectations of an eventual rate cut, and it picked up that September once the Fed's first cut after its hike regime was announced. With a selloff in the market in response to Trump's tariffs, HTD is down over 10% from its recent highs and trades near an 8% yield.

If a bear market lays ahead, HTD shows a portfolio of securities that weren't already overvalued and that can be reinvested at a good rate of return. As rate cuts have already been announced, albeit more slowly than they were hiked, over time this should support capital gains.

For the utilities in the portfolio, which are often fairly leveraged themselves, rate cuts should help them improve their interest expense and increase free cash flow to support the common stocks' dividend growth as well.

Risks

I expect most of the risk in the portfolio to be among the financials. In covering other investments, I've at times talked about the state of commercial real estate, which saw a surge of mortgages in 2021, following COVID stimulus and rate cuts to near-zero. Many of those loans have experienced delinquencies and needed to be restructured, especially last year, as the assets have been troubled by higher operating costs from inflation and lower-than-expected tenant occupancy.

A recent piece, considering the impact of tariffs, by CBRE observes:

Industrial and retail leasing demand likely will see the most direct impacts as consumer spending decreases and the flow of goods shifts. Other property types, such as multifamily, office and hotels, would experience secondary effects of slower growth, which would include less demand and new supply as construction costs rise.

Additionally, leasing decisions by commercial tenants can be delayed when they are considering what will need to be done in the U.S. (if the tariffs stick or if Trump is using them to make a deal). For the borrowers who need to rent to repay those mortgages, it's a tough position, and the lenders are left in a pickle if these loans on their balance sheet can't be repaid.

Even if it doesn't lead to a 2008-level crisis, it can depress earnings and reduce the dividend income provided by the shares.

Even a major bank like Wells Fargo (WFC) had to cut its dividend in response to COVID, and its quarterly distribution hasn't fully recovered. Newer macroeconomic setbacks that further weaken loans, which are already being tested, stand out to me as the clearest, most likely risk for such a portfolio.

These risks not only could cause dividend cuts by the issuers; an associated selloff could trigger margin calls on HTD's leverage and force them to lock in losses in order to maintain liquidity.

Conclusion

While the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund isn't the most exciting dividend fund or a portfolio with enormous upside ahead, it does have the right pieces for a simple response to the looming threat of a bear market that may last years ahead: reinvesting dividends. Drawing on businesses that provide an essential role in the economy, HTD should be able to collect and distribute its investment income in most scenarios, with additional upside provided by not only capital gains on a rate but improvements in the balance sheets of their holdings.

For those reasons, I consider HTD to be a decent 'Buy.'

data.title: Palantir: Upgrading To Buy, Tariffs Would Be A Win-Win (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've covered Palantir Technologies (PLTR) several times in the past few months with a consistently bearish tone highlighting extreme valuation risks with the stock. While valuation risks certainly remain, the stock has re-entered orbit with the recent market selloff, warranting a reevaluation of the investment case.

As a brief overview, Palantir sells enterprise software used in a variety of end markets. For years, the company was primarily a government defense contractor offering a mission-critical software known as Gotham. Gotham, similar to Palantir's other products Foundry and Artificial Intelligence Platform ("AIP"), specializes in connecting disparate data sources into one common backend feed and producing a simplified interface and better analytics capabilities. This feeds into Palantir's "Ontology" framework, which is a methodology of representing disparate data in a common format. It essentially allows operators to view a digital model of their organization in a seamless interface. Adding AIP and Ontology together gives operators an agent to use within Ontology, which has clear value-add for enterprises. The ROI is worth the spend and enterprises have been flooding to Palantir Bootcamps.

Given this context, the stock has been in a parabolic upswing for the past year. The Bootcamp strategy has been extremely successful to date and I see no reason for deceleration. The company's US enterprise segment has performed exceptionally well in 2024 and will continue this trend in 2025.

From the Q4 '24 reports, Palantir reported the following highlights:

  • U.S. revenue up 52% YoY and 12% QoQ to $558 million
    • U.S. commercial revenue grew 64% YoY and 20% QoQ to $214 million
    • U.S. government revenue grew 45% YoY and 7% QoQ to $343 million
  • Revenue grew 36% YoY and 14% QoQ to $828 million
  • Closed 129 deals of at least $1 million, 58 deals of at least $5 million, and 32 deals of at least $10 million
  • Closed a record-setting $803 million of U.S. commercial total contract value (“TCV”), up 134% YoY and 170% QoQ
  • U.S. commercial remaining deal value (“RDV”) of $1.79 billion, up 99% YoY and 47% QoQ
  • Customer count grew 43% YoY and 13% QoQ

While these results certainly highlight the company's traction, the issue comes when you look at the stock's market cap. For a company with $828m in quarterly revenue, even if it's growing at 40% annually, $180 billion is far too steep a price to pay. At this price, a 2% upward move in the stock represents a market cap increase of $3.6b, eclipsing full year 2024 revenue by nearly a billion dollars. The math does not add up.

Yet, with as sharp a selloff as we've seen the past few weeks, the stock valuation is in a better place than it was at the beginning of the year. Palantir actually stands to benefit from a higher tariff environment, which I'll make the case for below. Further, the ongoing inflection in its margin profile and profitability could lead the company to actually grow into this insane valuation in the coming years. With that in mind, I'll be upgrading Palantir from Sell to Buy in this article. The market has clearly demonstrated its willingness to pay a premium multiple for this stock, therefore I cannot continue recommending selling on a valuation basis alone. Despite clear valuation risks, I expect Palantir to continue to generate market-beating returns.

Tariffs and AIP Profitability

Trump's tariff gambit benefits Palantir in two primary ways. First, there's an important clause in the reciprocal tariff policy that US-derived components used in a final product will not be subject to tariffs. This gives the company an abundant incentive to deeply understand their global supply chains and pinpoint the exact tariff treatment of their goods. Luckily for Palantir, it has software that is purpose-built for this type of use case. Increasing supply chain complexity and the need to more deeply understand supply chains is strictly beneficial for an enterprise software company that simplifies these data feeds. Even with the recent tariff pause, companies will be on high alert for understanding their global supply chain. In this scope, knowledge is power, and Palantir empowers organizations with the full context of their operations.

Second, a higher-for-longer tariff environment incentivizes domestic manufacturing investment. This investment will be disproportionately directed toward robotics, AI, and automation so that goods can be made in America without rampant inflation. While I consider the investment in robotics machinery as a second-order effect of tariffs, I believe that investment in the software used to manage these devices is a third-order effect. Palantir, again, is in a prime position to pounce on this opportunity.

A defining feature of an exceptional business is that its products are resistant to this exact type of turmoil and uncertainty. I see the risks of a recessionary environment and geopolitical tensions as immaterial. Additionally, the process of reshored manufacturing operations increases demand for AIP.

Geopolitical uncertainty increases demand for Palantir software, supply chain complexity increases demand for Palantir software, factory automation increases demand for Palantir software... do you see the trend? The company is enjoying an outright explosion in demand during a historic AI driven bull run. Despite short term tariff-related volatility, the stock has held up exceptionally well even considering the clear valuation risks.

Additionally, I see little downside risk related to the recent defense department announcement of budget cuts. There is an enormous amount of wasteful spend in the defense department, but Palantir's software is mission-critical and drives efficiency. There would be no reason to cut spending on this software. In fact, it could lead to larger contracts over time as the defense department seeks to better understand its disparate operations and decrease total spend.

Investor Takeaway

My initial article on Palantir was a Buy at $16.57 where I stated there's a lot to love here. That sentiment remains, there is a lot to love and very little to dislike about Palantir fundamentally. The company is experiencing an explosion in demand and met this with an innovative go-to-market "bootcamp" strategy for customer onboarding. Annualized contract and remaining deal values are both booming, leading to an explosion in profitability and consistent expansions in the company's margin profile. The stock is becoming a free cashflow machine, leaving abundant cash to reinvest in future initiatives. Palantir has met the AI wave with open arms and there's very few companies better positioned to ride the wave.

Overall, despite the extremely elevated valuation and clear risks presented therein, I do believe Palantir will continue generating market-beating returns in the short-run and is worth a spot in a risk-loving investors' portfolio. Therefore, I'm upgrading Palantir to a Buy.

data.title: Tariff Panic? Why I'm Buying More Microsoft While Others Sell
data.description:
Like other tech giants - Apple (AAPL) and Amazon.com (AMZN) - I've also owned Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) since 2014. Although its growth isn’t as explosive as some of its peers in the Magnificent 7, at today's prices, I can’t help but see this as a perfect entry point.

With all the other high-growth tech stocks, you might be wondering if Microsoft is still a buy. Spoiler: my analysis for the company is a “strong buy” due to its AI advancements and its predictable income that translates to solid overall financials.

Quick Overview

Microsoft is a household name, so I’ll keep this intro short. Microsoft Corporation is a global company with a diversified portfolio of products. The entire company operates through three segments - Productivity and Business Processes (Office, LinkedIn, Dynamics), Intelligent Cloud (Azure, server products, GitHub), and More Personal Computing (Windows, Surface, Xbox). Moreover, Microsoft has made successful moves in cloud computing and investments in artificial intelligence, positioning it as a tech leader in enterprise solutions.

Recent Financials

Microsoft's Q2 ’25 financials reported solid numbers across the board. The top-line revenue increased by 12% year-over-year to $69.63 billion, while its operating income grew 17% to $31.65 billion - a clear indication that Microsoft is making significant progress despite economic uncertainties.

The company’s bottom line also experienced a 10% increase to $24.10 billion, translating to a quarterly EPS of $3.23, up from $2.93 in the same year-ago quarter. Without a doubt, this growth is a result of Microsoft’s positive momentum.

The same can be said if you look at the company’s segment revenues:

Its Intelligent Cloud segment reported 19% year-over-year growth, since the company’s investments in this segment are paying off as more businesses embrace “moving to the cloud”. Also, this means Microsoft’s Azure platform is keeping pace with its top competitors like AWS and Google Cloud.

The Productivity and Business Processes segment isn't doing too shabby either - it reported solid 14% growth on the backs of Microsoft 365 subscriptions and LinkedIn.

I’ve been a fan of subscription income since high school and wrote about it in my first book, The Financially Independent Millennial. I don’t know any better way to generate consistent earnings than hitting customers’ credit cards on a monthly basis. But I digress.

Microsoft’s More Personal Computing segment, however, came out with flat 0% growth, due to the 21% increase in Search and news advertising revenue, excluding traffic acquisition costs. This doesn’t surprise me as I can’t recently recall anyone buying or using a “Microsoft Surface” recently, except maybe on TV.

Despite that, Microsoft managed to boost its overall revenue by 12% Y.O.Y. I think this only means the company is successfully shifting from its old-school Windows business to where the real money is: Cloud services, subscriptions, and of course, AI.

5-YR MSFT Stock Price Performance

Over the past 5 years, Microsoft has an impressive 114.72% return with a remarkably consistent growth trajectory, which outpaces major market indices.

Although there have been “across the board” pullbacks thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs mandate, MSFT stock hit a 52-week high of $468.35 while establishing support above its 52-week low of $344.72. The pull-back is primarily driven by fear, short seller, and profit-taking events. I, too, have considered selling my MSFT stock, but, why?

From everything I’ve read, my conclusion is that tariffs can’t last forever - at least without setting off a disaster amounting to that of the Great Depression.

So yes, right now, while there’s lots of panic, I think we’re now starting to see a buying opportunity that we haven’t seen in years.

5-YR EPS Magnificent 7 Comparison

Looking at the graph, Microsoft’s EPS (orange line) has been on a steady growth trajectory over the past five years - the company has long established its position within the tech industry, so the stability makes sense.

We can see that META is the absolute leader when it comes to EPS figures. Actually, I pulled the trigger and initiated a position on the stock at the beginning of April, following my analysis on March 19, 2025.

That said, it doesn’t mean META is more of a buy.

Microsoft's consistently increasing EPS is also not something we should be overlooking. I mean, to me, the company operates with a figurative license to print money.

But for a clearer comparison, I’ve extracted the Magnificent 7’s 5-YR diluted EPS data.
