As you can see, Microsoft’s EPS is up 106.8% EPS over the past 5 years - that’s more than doubling its earnings per share. You might say that growth rate is moderate when we compare it to others in the group, but to me, I think it is exceptional for a company of Microsoft's maturity (and overall size).

At $12.41 in absolute EPS, Microsoft is the second most profitable company, right behind Meta among the Magnificent 7. This goes to show that management is highly effective at working its revenue and expense levers to drive sustainable profitability.

If we contrast that with, say, Tesla, who started from negative EPS, or NVIDIA, whose numbers were embryonic, Microsoft's EPS growth started from a positive earnings base of $6 per share.

Bottom line? Microsoft is better at building upon already solid profits, and this is what sets it apart from other peers who had to climb out of the red.

Potential Catalyst: Microsoft Dragon Copilot

According to a recent report, the global healthcare IT market was valued at $312.92 billion and projected to grow from $354 billion this year (2025) to $981.2 billion by 2033, reflecting a 15.7% CAGR.

Recently, Microsoft introduced Microsoft Dragon Copilot. This is the combination of Dragon Medical One [DMO] and DAX Copilot [DAX], and if synergy works in Microsoft’s favor, it could yield massive opportunities, allowing Microsoft to expand its footprint in the healthcare IT market.

For starters, Microsoft developed an AI assistant to address the clinical documentation burden, which is one of the biggest challenges in healthcare today. By combining Dragon Medical One’s dictation features with DAX Copilot’s ambient listening technology, healthcare providers could save a lot of time.

If it works out, Microsoft could open up a new vertical and capture greater market share in an industry that’s always been skeptical about technology adoption - but is now investing in AI solutions due to staffing shortages and burnout. Even if clinicians save 5 minutes per visit and feel less burned out, healthcare providers are more likely to stay in their jobs while patients will have a better experience.

Potential Risks

Although I’m bullish on Microsoft, I think the company may face a small, yet uphill battle in the cloud, despite its market dominance.

One example would be the potential slowdown in cloud adoption rates. That could impact Microsoft’s revenue growth, particularly for its Azure segments, which have been a primary revenue driver in recent years. The thing about Azure is that it’s difficult to use for retail customers - this is something AWS is better at. And, once enterprise customers explore multi-cloud strategies, Microsoft’s cloud growth numbers could experience headwinds.

Also, the increasing adoption of generative AI can be both an opportunity and a threat for Microsoft. Why? The company has made considerable investments in this space, and these same technologies have the potential to disrupt traditional enterprise software - Microsoft's bread and butter. Competitors could develop solutions to challenge Microsoft's products like Office and Windows. And, if that happens, it’ll likely erode the company's market position in this particular segment.

Macroeconomic factors also present concerns for Microsoft investors. Although the company has proven resilient during market downturns (no doubt about that), tariffs, persistent inflation, interest rate fluctuations, or even recession fears could impact enterprise IT customer demands. One perfect example is how Trump’s trade war has triggered a stock market selloff, wiping out 10 trillion dollars in the stock market, and reignited concerns about potential economic fallout.

Industry Experts’ Take on Microsoft Corporation

Bernstein analyst Mark Moerdler doubled down on Microsoft's potential in the AI space - he maintained his Buy rating with a $516 price target, as the tech giant continues to leverage its Azure platforms and AI to deliver growth.

The timing of his assessment is also notable, given the recent market disruption from Chinese AI startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). For starters, DeepSeek claimed to have developed a language model on par with OpenAI’s GPT-4 for ONLY $6 million - a fraction of what industry giants typically spend. The announcement sent tech stocks tumbling. In fact, everyone in the Magnificent 7 line up experienced a dip in stock prices.

However, seasoned analysts aren't buying DeepSeek’s hype. In fact, the Bank of America highlighted that DeepSeek’s R1 model likely builds upon foundational models like Meta’s Llama, which calmed investors down. They also mentioned that the actual infrastructure cost to develop these base models is far from $6 million, which is further supported by Meta's plans to increase 2025 CapEx by over 56% to $60-65 billion.

Wedbush's Dan Ives was even more direct, and called the market's reaction a "buying opportunity":

Source: Dan Ives

Moreover, Microsoft's positioning in AI isn't accidental. The company has poured a lot of money into cloud services, productivity tools, and various business applications, which can be incorporated with AI to improve operations.

Is MSFT Fairly Valued?

Magnificent 7

2025 Forward P/E

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT)

28.57

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META)

21.39

NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA)

32.76

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA)

108.75

Apple Inc. (AAPL)

27.46

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG)

17.99

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN)

30.86

S&P 500 Technology (Sector Average)

23.77

Based on the table above, Microsoft's valuation appears somewhat elevated compared to the S&P 500 Technology sector average of 23.77, which means the stock trades at a premium compared to the broader tech sector. That said, I think this justifies Microsoft's position in high-growth areas like cloud computing and AI integration.

But when you compare Microsoft to its MAG7 peers, it sits somewhere in the middle. And I think where the stock is today and its multiple, it only reflects investors’ confidence in its ability to print cash.

Is MSFT Stock a Buy?

Microsoft's predictable recurring revenue, strong balance sheet and investments in AI warrant a premium valuation. Considering these factors, alongside Microsoft's execution track record and diversified business model, today's valuation now appears more than reasonable for long-term investors seeking to add to or initiate exposure to the sector.

data.title: The Progressive Corporation: Earnings Have Exploded, Take Advantage Of Recent Price Drop And Buy
data.description:
America’s total insurance market is worth approximately $1.7 trillion, in 2025. The property and casualty (P&C) industry has seen a rocky road through volatile premiums, increased natural disasters, and disgruntled customers. The P&C industry had an underwriting loss of $21.2 billion in 2023. In 2024, the industry recorded an underwriting gain of $24.8 billion, marking the first full-year underwriting gain since 2020.

Progressive (NYSE:PGR) is an insurance holding company that writes insurance for personal auto, special personal lines, commercial, and property such as homeowners and renters. The company employs over 40,000 people globally and has offices across six continents. PGR has 16.73% market share for auto insurance, which is the second largest in the United States.

The share price has varied between $289 and $255 in the first few weeks of April 2025, as investors ponder how to proceed amidst the Trump tariffs and international backlash. Even with recent drops, PGR has gained 36% over the last year and 257% over 5 years. There are many controversial economic and political topics lingering at the moment, and this is one of the several reasons why I rate PGR a buy. The company had a record year in net premiums and policies added. They have successfully increased advertising spend while generating massive gains in net income over a few years span. Earnings have exploded, while valuation has dropped at the same time. The stock has also tremendously outpaced its peers.

2024: Best Year in 87-Year History

In their Q4 presentation, PGR stated that 2024 may have been the best year in the firm's long 87-year history. Net premiums written, and net premiums earned both increased 21% YoY. Over the last 4 years, they have both gained on average by over 16% annually and are at the peaks in early 2025. Underwriting margin, the difference between premiums earned and claims paid, was up over double in 2024 to 11.2% and the company’s best since 2020.

The combined ratio, a key performance indicator for insurance companies, is measured as total incurred losses and expenses divided by earned premiums. PGR was down 6.1 points to 88.8% for 2024, meaning their profitability went up substantially.

Personal lines are the majority of business for PGR. In terms of policies in force, commercial lines account for under 4% of business. Auto and special lines reported over 30 million policies in force in 2024, with property just over 3.5 million policies. Total personal lines grew 18% in the last year and are up 41% since 2020.

Income & Expenses

Revenue has nearly doubled for progressive in the last 5 years and has reached an all-time high of $75.34 billion in 2024, up 21% from the year prior. Operating expenses have almost equally gone up, with policy payouts having doubled in the same timeframe with the number of claims continuing to rise. Policy payouts actually rose much less in 2024, but it was policy acquisition costs that steepened.

Advertising spending hit a record high of nearly $3.5 billion in 2024, up 186.8% from the $1.22 billion it spent in 2023. This spend on advertising was in an attempt to gain market share. Successfully, PGR gained 1.5% market share in auto insurance. This sounds small but is really a decent jump with the number of insurers available to consumers. The uptick in advertising expense has showcased worthwhile as you can see the largest percent gains in auto and property policies seen over 5 years.

The above chart is competitor Allstate (ALL). While ALL increased their advertising spend last year, they were not able to as efficiently increase their number of policies with it. Perhaps “Flo” from Progressive commercials is more effective than the average consumer realizes.

Income Statement Continued

PGR has seen an astounding jump in net income and EBITDA. Net income is up 11.5x in just two years, now at $8.48 billion. EBITDA for 2024 was $11.27 billion, 6.5x higher than 2022. PGR has mastered their formula in increasing advertising spend just enough to ultimately help enhance its bottom-line.

Technical Analysis

The stock price fall off amidst the tariff news has driven PGR below its 50-day average. I get it, this isn’t a good thing in normal circumstances, but as we all know, this was an abnormal week and just about every stock out, there is in the same boat. With the drop, the stock's RSI (blue graph) is now at 36. Market conditions are different right now, but you can see that any time RSI has been at that level in the last year, the stock has rebounded nicely and timely.

Valuation & Earnings

Valuation wise, it holds a P/E ratio of 17.87, which is double the sector average, and has a price to book of 6.57 against the sector median of 1.14. But if you look deeper, their valuation is not as rough as it indicates. Their PEG is .17, whereas the sector is .61 on average. PGR is still valued well below the general market, and in fact is seeing a P/E ratio (red line above) of nearly half of what it was a year ago. It is not overly common to see a stock gain like PGR has, all while seeing valuation drop.

The reasoning behind it is the massive spike in EPS. In 2024, PGR saw $14.08 annual EPS. 2022 and 2023 resulted in $3.98 and $6.16 respectively. The earnings growth seen here is remarkable and is possibly the biggest positive moving forward for PGR. The company will report Q1 2025 earnings in April and is anticipated to report earnings of $4.60 per share, which would be 23.3% growth YoY.

Peers

PGR has outperformed all its publicly owned peers by quite a lot. To no surprise, the stock began to really separate itself in 2024, while they made huge strides in net premiums, policies, and financials across the board. State Farm, as a privately owned firm, is another big competitor and is the nation’s leader in auto market share. State Farm gained just 0.5% market share in 2024, 1% less than PGR.

Economically Set

It’s worth briefly touching on the economic impact for PGR, as the world gets hit by updated news on an almost daily basis. I think the P&C insurance industry is in a great place compared to many others. They are essentially unaffected by tariffs and any geopolitical affairs. Auto and property insurance is still needed during recessionary periods, so demand and number of policies should remain unharmed for the most part. I think over the next several months to a year, while economic concerns get sorted out, the insurance industry is not a bad place for investors.

Conclusion

Well, it has been a tumultuous few weeks in the markets, and PGR is a culprit like the rest. I think it’s a good opportunity to add PGR to your portfolio during lingering uncertain times. The P&C industry is relatively safe compared to others, while we have unclear signs ahead. Bottom-line financial strength and advancements in total policies and net premiums are booming for PGR. If earnings continue to grow anywhere near their recent rate, the company will be in a great position. I like PGR as a buy.

data.title: ICL Group: Growing Profitability Coupled With Keen Value To Drive Shares Northward
data.description:
Intro

ICL Group Ltd. (ICL) came across our desk as a value setup that has been holding its own amid the recent volatility in financial markets. This company is an Israeli-based specialty and chemicals company that produces and distributes bromine, potash, and phosphate, among other products, to international markets in the fertilizer and agricultural industries. Its operations in Israel have been affected because of the war, but management has done an excellent job in minimizing disruptions, all things remaining equal. To this point, although the company missed top & bottom-line estimates in its fourth quarter results (announced at the end of February this year), shares are up approximately 18% year to date despite US tariffs wreaking havoc on markets in recent sessions.

Bullish Technicals

ICL's long-term chart looks compelling. Just before the turn of the year, shares registered a MACD crossover, resulting in a strong upturn in ICL's 10-month moving average in recent months. MACD crossovers are particularly noteworthy on long-term charts, especially if they take place in oversold territory. The reason is the indicator acts as both an oscillator & a trend-following system incorporating a moving-average approach. Therefore, to aid the long-term bullish case here, if ICL's 10-month moving average could cross above its 40-month counterpart, this would provide further evidence that a sustained bullish run lies ahead for ICL.

Cheap Valuation

ICL's encouraging technicals stem from the positive relationship between the company's profitability trends & valuation. Despite growth struggles in recent times, earnings growth is expected to hit close to 9% in fiscal 2025 followed by 11%+ year-over-year growth in the following year. These projections sit very well with us when delving into ICL's present valuation. As we see below, the company's sales, assets & cash flow are all cheaper at present (trailing values) compared to their 5-year counterparts. Therefore, irrespective of the company's poor top-line growth rates in recent times, as long as there are enough sales to generate consistent cash flow, then this very same cash flow can be used to increase the asset count, which reinitiates ICL's sales cycle once more.

Multiple 12-Month Trailing 5-Year Average
Price/Earnings (GAAP) 17.94 -
Price/Sales 1.07 1.56
Price/Book 1.28 2.21
Price/Cash-Flow 4.98 8.89

Free Cash Flow Strength

Free cash flow is the most important metric in investing, bar none. In ICL's most recent fiscal year, free cash flow came in at $0.59 per share, resulting in a trailing free cash-flow multiple of 9.59. Suffice it to say, that the company's 10%+ free-cash-flow yield, strong earnings conversion combined with renewed earnings growth is an excellent foundation for sustained gains in ICL going forward. The below charts demonstrate how historic free cash-flow gains have led to bullish cycles in the potash producer over time.

GAAP Dividend Payout Ratio

The GAAP dividend payout ratio is the quickest way to see if the current payout is affordable. To this point, management has been distributing 50% of adjusted net profit to shareholders in the form of a quarterly dividend. Therefore, whereas adjusted net profit came in at $0.38 per share in fiscal 2024, core GAAP profit came in at $0.32 for the fiscal year. Although ICL's adjusted numbers are fine to run off (due to the company's excellent cash-flow conversion rates), ICL's core GAAP dividend payout ratio of 59.4% comes in above average when compared to the sector (40.71%)

Is this a concern with respect to dividend affordability? We do not believe so. Above-average payout ratios can be fundamentally bullish for a company especially when one considers ICL's valuation. Let me explain.

ICL's stated ROC comes in at 5.77%. However, since the company requires less of its earnings (retained earnings) to produce the above returns, this demonstrates better internal efficiency due to being able to generate its returns from a lower capital base.

Projected Forward-Looking Rate Of Return

Therefore, even if ICL doesn't hit its present consensus EPS target for fiscal 2025, given the company's keen valuation and high return on incremental capital (ROIC), we could easily see shareholders earning a double-digit return in fiscal 2025.

We calculate the rate of return utilizing the following formula

Rate of Return = G + D/M

Where

G = Projected annual earnings growth rate

D = GAAP dividend payout ratio

M = Trailing GAAP Price To Earnings Ratio

When we insert our values into the formula, we see

ICL's Projected Rate Of Return = 8.75% + 59.4%/17.94 = 12.06%

Balance Sheet

At the end of fiscal 2024, ICL held $442 million in cash & ST investments, $1.46 billion in receivables & $1.62 billion in inventory. Furthermore, the company's net debt to adjusted EBITDA stood at 1.2 with reported book value coming in at $5.72 billion. A few takeaways from the above trends.

From a liquidity standpoint (current ratio of 1.54), ICL's short-term liabilities remain well covered by some distance. Furthermore, the company's net debt ($1.87 billion) continues to fall, resulting in a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.39 (The five-year average comparable comes in at 0.55). Therefore, despite ICL's growth struggles in recent years, through solid cash-flow generation, liquidity has remained intact and solvency has improved meaningfully at the firm.

Therefore, given the sizable amount of 'Net Property, Plant & Equipment' and lack of intangible assets on ICL's balance sheet, we see the company's trailing book multiple of 1.28 being very attractive to investors from this standpoint.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we believe the ICL Group has plenty of upside due to bullish technicals, robust free cash flow trends, growing profitability, and a balance sheet that is growing stronger by the day. Let's see if the stock can continue to buck the trend and take out its February highs ($6.45) sometime in the near term. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: 5 Key Investment Trends To Watch For The Next Decade
data.description:
In case you've been living under a rock, its' been a busy week in the markets.

Last Wednesday, the Trump Administration announced a whole slew of proposed tariffs on global trade, and since that point, stock indices around the world have been in free fall:

(Update - since then, tariffs have been partially reversed, which has led to quite the bounce)

With a big move like this, it's common to think about how policy changes will impact your money in the short term. Is there more pain on the way? Will the tariffs get worse? Is inflation headed higher?

The real answer is this: Who knows? Things may head higher - or lower - in the short run. As everyone is fond of saying, it can be hard to time the market.

However, keeping your mind focused on the long term is paramount. 10 years from now, we'd argue that this selloff will hardly register as a blip, while key trends impacting consumer tastes, demand, and other structural changes to society will drive enormous appreciation for select companies and industries.

Instead of fretting about the market, positioning yourself now to take advantage of these trends is key.

But which stocks are poised to benefit?

Today, in this article, we'll take a step back from the market carnage and outline 5 key trends that we believe could drive investment returns over the next decade.

We'll also outline who we think the key 'winners' of these trends could be.

Sound good?

Let's dive in.

Trend 1: Content, Media, and Communication

In the inaugural episode of his podcast 'The Portal' back in 2019, mathematician and portfolio manager Eric Weinstein had on Peter Thiel as a guest.

While there were a great number of topics that the duo could have discussed, much time was devoted to the idea that base research in physics and the hard sciences has largely stagnated since the 1970s. Following a long period of innovation in physics that led to nuclear weapons and energy, lasers, radios, rocket engines, and more, the pair argued that the modern world - save the screens we now all use on a daily basis - looks much like it did in 1970.

Our houses look the same, our appliances are all the same, etc:

This is a reasonable conclusion, and we agree - more investment into the hard sciences is needed to drive society forward and improve living standards.

However, what this argument fails to grasp is that 'screens', which were the point of derision in this conversation, have fundamentally changed society, even if the built environment looks the same.

In just a few short decades, the internet has rebuilt everything about how humans communicate, which has led to a virtual revolution when it comes to almost everything. Politics. Medicine. Consumer tastes. Culture. How we see ourselves. All of these have been re-calibrated by the internet and the fact that humans all over the globe can now communicate instantly in a way that was never possible before.

Over the last few years, we've seen a consistent blending of three distinct things that all used to be separate - Communication, Media, and Content. Instead of writing letters to - or calling - friends, we send them TikToks and YouTube videos. As it turns out, entertainment, when combined with social interaction, is highly engaging. Perhaps more saso than traditional channels. You can see this, as many 'traditional' media firms have needed to move onto these platforms to retain relevance.

At the same time, most of the world's direct communication has shifted to these same platforms. Instagram DMs, WhatsApp's, and Facebook Messages now form the backbone of global chatter.

As the time that we spend on socializing, entertainment, and news has all blended into one, a few companies have benefited, including Meta (META) and Google (GOOG).

Now, given that we see these trends being reinforced by a generation that smokes, drinks, and has less sex less than any other (and uses these platforms more than any other) we'd argue that the market opportunity here is massive:

The more attention you take up, the more valuable your platform. Thus, we're structurally bullish on META, GOOG, Reddit (RDDT) and whoever buys TikTok in the coming decade. Similarly, if Discord ever goes public, we'll be all over that.

We expect that losers from this trend will include traditional media firms that used to own their audiences, like:

  • Nexstar Media Group (NXST)
  • Fox (FOX)
  • Comcast (CMCSA)

Other losers will likely also include Marketing Agencies like Omnicom (OMC) and WPP (WPP), who are cut out of the middle by platforms' vertical integration, and companies like Shutterstock (SSTK) and Getty Images (GETY) that now have to compete with UGC.

In sum, we see the future of communication and media focused more on the influencer economy & short form content, and less on more traditional content & delivery mechanisms.

Trend 2: Subscriptions, Marketplaces, and Platforms

Another trend we've noticed is that key providers across consumer spend are becoming increasingly focused on recurring revenue. This isn't new.

What is new is how many of these recurring revenues are increasingly being attached to some of the largest marketplaces and platforms that are already quite sticky. This seems to be a prevalent consumer trend, and we expect it will continue and consolidate over time.

Lets' look at an average consumer budget to explain what we're taking about.

First, potential sources of income:

  • Salary
  • Dividends
  • Rental Income

Now, the expenses:

  • Rent / Mortgage
  • Utilities
  • Food
  • Staples
  • Transportation
  • Travel
  • Entertainment
  • Discretionary Spend
  • Insurance
  • Investing

Some of these categories, like Rent, Utilities, and Insurance aren't impacted by this dynamic. We expect businesses like Verizon (VZ), ConEd (ED), and UnitedHealthcare (UNH) (and most landlords) to be firmly unchanged within a decade:

However, a significant chunk of the consumer budget goes towards things like food, transportation, staples, discretionary spend, entertainment, and travel. These are consumer segments that we see rapidly consolidating into a few large players that extract as much value as possible for investors, combining the value of a marketplace with the profitability of a subscription model.

For things like Food and Transportation, companies like Uber (UBER), and Grab (GRAB) provide the largest marketplaces for delivery and mobility, are rapidly gaining global share, and have attached subscription businesses. Uber One, anyone?

For Staples and Discretionary Spend, Amazon's (AMZN) Prime membership is the largest subscription business in the world, on top of the company's unbelievable marketplace of goods and services.

Finally, in the entertainment sphere, companies like Spotify (SPOT) and Roblox (RBLX) run massive consumer marketplace platforms for gaming and audio content, and have subscription businesses.

This intersection of marketplace flywheel and subscription revenue is incredibly cash-generative, and should allow leading platforms to become completely dominant in years' time.

The winners are largely those we've listed above, and broadly, the losers - we expect - will be smaller competitors in each of those categories.

Lyft (LYFT) seems likely to become a long-suffering casualty of Uber. Smaller competitors to Amazon, like Target (TGT), are already seeing flat sales growth. Firms like Electronic Arts (EA) are struggling to compete with Roblox, both on users and on growth. We're not saying that any of these companies will go out of business, but we're expecting that they'll face serious, serious headwinds in the future.

Plus, we expect that hundreds of thousands of smaller businesses will also face threats from this consolidation.

Trend 3: FinTech

The next trend we're excited about has to do with how consumers interact with money. For the longest time, managing your personal finances could be a chore, with different providers for different functions. Older generations may still choose to manage their finances this way, with a bank for saving and spending, a broker for investing, a third provider for insurance, a fourth provider for a mortgage, etc.

In the current era, there are up-and-coming companies that are rolling all of these services into one, which reduces complexity, improves the overall experience, and allows for better financial decisions on the part of users.

SoFi Technologies (SOFI) is one. Robinhood (HOOD) is another. Nubank (NU) is a popular option in South America.

While not all of these new companies provide all of the requisite products, the idea is the same - in a world of increasing complexity, it makes sense to have all (or most) of your money marching in lockstep in a single place:

If the value prop of this as a user doesn't immediately smack you, then we're not sure how to explain it any better.

From a 'trends' point of view, however, we see this as an existential threat to financial managers and wealth advisors. As older generations begin to die off and wealth gets passed down to younger, more tech savvy generations, we expect that many of them won't want to pay exorbitant rates or management fees. Sure, there are some estates which are complex from a tax or legal perspective, but for the most part, financial management is about getting your excess capital working for you. Why bother with the 1% annual fee?

For those that do choose to pay a fee, we expect that will be for complex management. Asset managers, P/E, etc.

Thus, we see a dual impact - Wealth Management headwinds, and Asset Management tailwinds. As in, the creators of financial products will likely see a boost as they continue to hoover up passive cash.

Firms like BlackRock (BLK) and Blackstone (BX) will likely see a boost from deposits and compounding AUM, while companies more focused on the personal relationship, like Morgan Stanley (MS), may face issues.

Finally, we also expect that smaller, single-service providers will struggle. In a world where consumers expect financial apps to become increasingly 'all in one', we see regional banks (KRE) as being particularly uncompetitive.

To sum up the last three sections and trends, we expect continued entrenchment of the smartphone.

Given its' ability to be the 'remote control' to your life, we're anticipating that leading, sticky, app-based businesses that monopolize consumer spend, attention, and money will win big, and keep winning as the world becomes more digitized.

Here's a quick summary of the winners and losers we see so far:

Category Winners Losers
Communications META, GOOG, RDDT FOX, NSXT, CMCSA, OMC, WPP, SSTK, GETY
Marketplaces / Subscriptions UBER, GRAB, RBLX, SPOT, AMZN, ABNB LYFT, TGT, BBY, EA, WMG, Assorted public/private Retailers & service providers
FinTech SOFI, ALLY, HOOD, NU, BLK, BX, KKR, BN MS, LPLA, RJF, Smaller banks like those in KRE

Now, let's take a look at real estate - something that exists entirely in the plane of the physical.

Trend 4: Demography & Real Estate

The fourth trend we're thinking about is how Real Estate investing will work in the future.

For the longest time, Real Estate has done incredibly well. Even today, young people looking to strike it rich are often told to go into real estate, given that there's been such a tremendous amount of wealth creation in that asset class over the last 70 years.

Even when adjusted for inflation, the price of the average house has increased substantially since the 50's and 60's, and those who have had exposure (often leveraged) to this trend have done well. The combination of easy leverage, constrained supply, infrequent valuations, and forced long hold times has led to a perfect storm of capital appreciation.

But will that hold for the next 70 years?

In our view, the gains we've experienced are set to materially slow down, or even reverse, in the coming years.

Have a look at this chart, which shows expected population growth in various countries between now and 2100:

As you can see, in some developed countries, like the US, the growth rates slow down considerably. In other developed countries, like Brazil, China, and Japan, populations are expected to decline.

At the end of the day, real estate is priced based on supply and demand. In 30 years, if there's the same number of buildings and 70% of the people, as will be the case in some of these places, then you're looking at lower prices for real estate.

Of course, it isn't quite that simple. As we'll talk about in the next section, we believe that local markets play a bigger role on pricing than national level trends. Cities will probably continue to do well, given that most of the economic growth in the coming years will take place in cities.

However, this does imply a wipeout for rural property holders that aren't focused on rural activities, like agriculture. Live in a small town in upstate New York? Or Ohio? We'd anticipate that many of these places will slowly turn into West-Virginia like coal towns within the next 20-30 years.

Not a place to buy a property with leverage (a mortgage), in our view.

From an investment POV, all of this is to say that real estate and REITs may not be a bad place to allocate over the next 20 years, but location matters. If you're looking to buy, be critically aware of the concentration we expect will occur, and the inertia of, property in big cities going forward. For REITs, this means looking for companies with property in places with clear global inertia.

We'd rather buy a mid-quality REIT based in New York City, as opposed to a diversified REIT with property spread all over the Midwest.

Trend 5: Lifestyle Arbitrage

Finally, the last trend we'll touch on doesn't have a direct corollary with the public stock market, but it matters a great deal from a personal finance and investing perspective.

One of our strong beliefs is that we are in the very early innings of a global re-alignment in terms of pricing with respect to quality of life.

In the past, cities and countries that you weren't born in were difficult to access on a permanent or semi-permanent basis.

Born in the U.S. in 1950 and want to live in China? Good luck.

However, with the advent of better communications technology (see trend 1) and remote work, we think times are changing. For one, as a result of social media, we believe that more than ever most people are seeing themselves as global citizens, as opposed to 'local' citizens. Politically, this has been clear for a while. If you live in a small town in Spain, U.S. federal politics have no real impact on you, but more and more, they're a fixture of your daily conversation with acquaintances.

"Can you believe what the American President did?"

We're starting to see this happen with respect to global lifestyles as well.

If you were living in the inner city in Chicago in 1980, there wasn't a real impetus to move. Your family was likely there, along with your work, your friends, etc.

Maybe you'd vacation in 'nice' places, but you'd always go back.

Now, keeping up with far-flung friends and family is easier than ever, and platforms like Instagram have made people realize just what kind of a deal they're getting.

Why live in Chicago, when you can live in Bali for a fraction of the price?

Where would you rather live?

This arbitrage has existed for a while on an economic basis. Buying a property in Manhattan will cost you a lot more than buying a property in Buffalo. Why? The earnings potential is much greater, and the property prices reflect that.

However, going forward, given the increased global awareness that we're all now experiencing, (and the increased concentration of wealth in the hands of people who are retiring or location independent), we're expecting that prices for goods, services, and property in some places will adjust higher or lower based on lifestyle as well.

For example, the following is a map that shows 'quality of life' metrics based on global user input, compiled by international living site Numbeo:

The numbers may surprise you. Some places score better than others, both within the U.S. and abroad.

Let's take a quick comparison between two major global cities - New York City, USA, and Cape Town, South Africa:

It may surprise you to find out that Cape Town significantly outperforms New York on a number of metrics, including traffic, Climate, Pollution, Health Care, and more, while only lagging slightly on Safety (For reference, inequality in South Africa drives a lot of crimes of desperation, which is why you're more likely to have your purse stolen than in New York - most of the crime is non-violent).

At the same time, if you look at each place, it's not hard to grasp this difference in quality of life:

Again, where would you rather live?

And yet, when you look at each city's cost of living, you get wildly different facts and figures:

There are barriers to moving, for sure. Some countries with a high quality of life don't have flexible visa policies.

However, we expect that the gaps between quality of life and pricing for global locations will close in the coming decades, starting this decade.

From an investing perspective, this has implications for real estate, of course, but also for services that help people live a more global lifestyle.

This includes communications tools, like Facetime (AAPL) Teams (MSFT) and Zoom (ZM), but also more practical tools, like Airbnb (ABNB) for housing, Wise (OTCPK:WPLCF) for Payments, Expedia (EXPE) for flights, etc.

We're structurally bullish on these firms that facilitate global mobility amongst the remote and retired class.

Summary

So, there you have it - 5 key investment trends to watch for the coming decade.

As society shifts and consumer preferences change and adapt to global circumstances, we think there that these are some trends that will only become more pronounced in the coming years.

We're hoping that this article was helpful in clarifying what those might be, and how you might invest well, or avoid making big bets in the wrong places.

More than anything, stay safe out there!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Gap Between A 'Hold' And A 'Buy' Has Been Bridged
data.description:
Historically speaking, I have not been a very big fan of companies in the clothing retail space. Intense competition and low margins, not to mention the uncertainty that comes with the whims and wishes of people from a fashion perspective, is not generally attractive to a value investor like me that likes safer opportunities. But every so often, I will find a firm in an industry that I think is worth considering. That happens to be the case today with The Gap (NYSE:GAP).

Fundamentally speaking, the picture for The Gap has been a bit mixed in recent years. Revenue has been lumpy. But the good news is that management has achieved significant margin improvement and has seen cash flows grow materially as a result. The business continues to transition toward a franchise model that should, in the long run, benefit shareholders. On top of this, the company has eliminated debt and now has a good chunk of capital in the form of cash. When you add on top of this the fact that shares are attractively priced, including compared to some other similar enterprises, and I believe that it is deserving of a ‘buy’ rating.

Trying The Gap on

The management team at The Gap describes the company as an omni-channel retailer that focuses on selling directly to customers through its stores and online. It has company-operated locations and franchise stores. It has its own websites and even certain arrangements with third parties. At present, the firm's company-operated locations are located throughout the US, Canada, Japan, and Taiwan. In 2022, it entered into an agreement to allow Baozun, Inc. the ability to operate Gap China and Gap Taiwan stores and the websites associated with those countries. While the transaction for its Gap China stores closed in January of 2023, it is still waiting for its locations in Taiwan to transfer over.

The firm has a number of major clothing brands under its belt. This naturally includes Gap, which operates as an adult apparel and accessories brand that also caters to babies, pregnant women, children, and more. On top of this, the company has ownership over Old Navy, Banana Republic, and Athleta. This structure gives it a diverse revenue stream and grants the company the opportunity to experiment with different concepts without risking the whole farm.

Over the past few years, The Gap has undergone an interesting journey. From 2022 to 2023, for instance, revenue for the business plummeted from $15.62 billion to $14.89 billion. Part of this decline was because of a 2% drop in comparable store sales. This drop was in spite of the fact that the company had one extra operating week in 2023 than it did in 2022. It also came in spite of the fact that the business went from having 3,352 stores in operation to having 3,560. Most of this drop can be attributed to the transition of its Gap China business from being one that was company operated to being franchised. Then, in 2024, revenue popped to $15.09 billion. During this time, the company benefited from a 3% rise in comparable store sales. It also enjoyed a slight increase in the number of locations to 3,569.

It would be helpful to discuss the change in location count that the company has seen in recent years. This is because part of the company is shrinking while the other part is growing. From 2022 to 2024, the number of company-operated locations declined from 2,685 to 2,506. However, the number of franchise locations ballooned from 667 to 1,063. The advantage to focusing more on franchising operations is that margins tend to be larger. This is because the company is generating revenue not from the sales of the locations, but from a portion of the revenue that has very little in the way of cost structure to it. The downside is that this has a negative effect on overall revenue. But every company that follows this path hopes that the strong profit margins will generate more in the way of profit and cash flow in the long run than having low margin stores operating.

This has been just one part of the changes that the company has undergone. In April 2023, management announced a broader restructuring plan. This involved reducing the firm's workforce by roughly 1,800. It also involved other measures as well. For instance, the business also sold off some of its assets, including one building for $76 million. This plan followed an earlier one that saw, from the beginning of 2020 to the end of 2023, the business closed 344 Gap and Banana Republic stores throughout North America. This was net of any new stores opened.

The impact on the bottom line has been tremendous. In the chart above, you can see the company's gross profit margin, and you can also see its operating expenses as a percent of revenue. Clearly, this bold initiative has paid off. As a result of all of these changes, the company went from generating a net loss of $202 million in 2022 to generating a net profit of $844 million last year. Other profitability metrics also improved. Operating cash flow jumped from $607 million to $1.49 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $365 million to $1.49 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business expanded from $476 million to $1.62 billion.

These increased cash flows have also drastically improved the company's balance sheet. Back in 2022, the firm had net debt of $621 million. But by the end of 2024, The Gap had eliminated its net debt, turning to a net cash position of $1.10 billion. Moving forward, the company also has its sight set on even stronger financial performance. After tariffs were initially announced on China, Canada, and Mexico, but before this newest round of tariffs occurred, management claimed that revenue for 2025 would be about 1% to 2% higher than it was in 2024. However, operating income was expected to rise by between 8% and 10%. The firm would spend about $600 million on capital projects, but ultimately would see net store closures associated with its company-operated locations of 35. This all indicates to me that the company is still a work in progress and should see additional benefits as time goes on.

The issue of tariffs does raise concern in the near term. In the final quarter earnings call that the company gave for 2024, management talked briefly about tariffs. They said that less than 10% of their products as measured by revenue ultimately come from China. And Mexico and Canada combined account for less than 1%. However, this does not factor in the impact that other global tariffs will have on the company. Certainly, the company imports most, if not all, of the products that it sells. I wouldn't be surprised if management comes out with a downward revision in its next earnings call. But only time will tell if that prediction turns out to be right.

Using the historical results that we have seen for 2024 and 2023, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Especially using the 2024 figures, the business looks appealing from a valuation standpoint. Furthermore, compared to other similar enterprises, shares are attractively priced for the most part. In the table below, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, I found that two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than The Gap is. And when using the price to operating cash flow scenario, only one of the companies was cheaper.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
The Gap 8.5 4.8 3.8
Urban Outfitters (URBN) 10.9 8.8 6.0
Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) 6.8 5.5 2.9
American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) 6.5 4.5 2.3
The Buckle (BKE) 9.3 7.5 5.9
Guess? (GES) 17.2 5.8 5.9

Takeaway

As things stand, I must say that I am rather impressed with The Gap at this time. The business is cheap, especially compared to most similar enterprises. Management has made tremendous progress over the past few years at transforming the company. Margins have improved and cash flows have shot up even as revenue has declined. Add all of this together, combined with the strong balance sheet that the company currently has, and I do believe that even with the tariff uncertainty that could impair results, the company is cheap enough and of a high enough quality to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: AGG: Dollar Recycling System Halt May Trigger Largest Ever Bond Market Crash
data.description:
In January, I outlined my view that the surge in gold compared to bonds signaled Treasuries may be repricing according to debt monetization risk in "AGG: Gold Is Signaling A Debt Crisis In Bonds." The Aggregate bond ETF (AGG) rose temporarily over the past three months as the equity market shifted in a defensive direction. However, to the surprise of many, AGG has crashed over the past week, following the stock market's lower. AGG is about a half percent lower than when I published that bearish outlook.

On Monday, amid a spike in equity market volatility, I offered an extra note explaining why I am not in a rush to buy the dip in either bonds or stocks, saying:

While it is too early to say with certainty, if the US dollar loses that status (Monetary hegemony), I do not think the S&P 500 will recover compared to inflation. The "sign" that this is occurring will likely be if US Treasury bonds lose value or fail to rise while stocks crash, indicating a foreign exodus of US financial assets. This is not yet confirmed, as Treasuries generally rose last week; however, if we see funds, like TLT, decline or stagnate while the S&P 500 falls, I would caution against dip-buying because that may signal a long-term collapse of the US dollar recycling system that has driven its historical recovery.

Five days later, this is precisely what we've seen. The long-term bond ETF (TLT) has crashed by a staggering 7% this week, while the more diverse AGG is about 3% lower. Crucially, the US Dollar Index ETF (UUP) is also down 3%, indicating a substantial shift against the dollar, implied by the foreign selling of US bonds.

Although my outlook remains speculative, I see this as a strong sign that AGG and other bonds are repricing for debt monetization risk. AGG may be on its first leg of a much lower move if true. That said, AGG is historically seen as a risk-off defensive bet, potentially encouraging many investors to relocate into it out of equities.

Why Stocks and Bonds May Fall Together

It is rare for stocks and bonds to fall together, and this shift is a critical signal for both. Regarding tariffs, foreign governments have limited ability to penalize the US, given the US is not dependent on exports. However, the US government is highly dependent on foreign investment to finance its deficit, particularly at a lower-than-reasonable interest rate given its objectively poor solvency metrics.

There is ample speculation regarding the foreign selling of Treasuries. Given that the US dollar is falling against long-term Treasuries and stocks, some foreign selling of Treasuries is likely to occur. However, we cannot know with certainty which countries may be doing this, as the data is reported monthly and is delayed.

AGG's correlations with other major sectors are shifting quickly. Historically, AGG has had a robust inverse correlation with the US dollar index fund UUP. This is logical as higher interest rates cause the dollar's relative value to rise while directly lowering bond prices. AGG's correlation with stocks is often negative because lower stock prices signal a decline in inflation and interest rates. However, AGG's relationship with the S&P 500 bounced up over the past week, and though not notable, is not negative. AGG's correlation to gold also bounced, but has trended negatively over the past two years for reasons detailed in my previous AGG article. See below:

These patterns are also low in absolute terms. However, they are lagged by one month. The past week has seen a dramatic shift in markets, making the direction of these correlations particularly important.

The most notable shift is the slight positive correlation between the US dollar and AGG and between SPY (S&P 500) and AGG. This tells me that foreign activity is a key factor driving changes in the bond market. That is illustrated if we look at the price change of both over the past week:

I speculate that China is selling its US dollar-denominated bonds. However, knowing who is selling is impossible because many factors drive currency changes. For example, many believe we can see which currencies are rising the fastest as a sign of dollar selling pressure.

This week's greatest major currency shift was the sharp rise in the Swiss Franc, which has risen 5.5% since Monday. The Czech Krone is also up around 4.5%, while the Danish Krone is up about 4%. The Euro is also up around 4%, while the British Pound is up only ~2%. The Japanese Yen and Taiwanese Dollar are up about 2.5%. The Chinese Yuan is only up around 30 bps.

Currency changes do not tell us who is selling Treasuries, but where the best alternative to the dollar lies. Based on this data, the Swiss Franc is likely arguably the most fiscally, economically, and geopolitically stable country. Although not as powerful, Denmark and the Czech Republic have similar attributes to Switzerland.

Japan has stated that it is not planning to sell Treasuries to retaliate against tariffs. That may or may not be true, and I would not expect Japan to admit it if true, as that could cause Trump to escalate. Japan, China, and the UK are the largest Treasury holders. Either of those three, or private owners, may be selling. However, I suspect China because its currency has not changed. I think China is selling Treasuries and buying other currencies, such as Swiss Francs, with those proceeds. China and Japan are very export-focused, so they have ample reason to make such exchanges to avoid appreciating their currencies.

Can the US Dollar Bond Collapse Spiral?

There is also the basis trade margin call issue. Around $800B may be in a trade wherein hedge funds buy Treasuries and short interest rate futures, profiting from their convergence over time. However, poor Treasury auctions this week, potentially due to a lack of foreign demand and investment funds, caused this strategy to fail and trigger margin calls.

Although this is important in the short term, I do not think it's as critical as the unwinding of the US dollar recycling system. This is the crux of my bearish view on stocks and bonds. Fundamentally, the US government can maintain lower interest rates despite a sizable fiscal deficit and debt level because of high foreign demand for US investments. This may also be why US equity valuations are 50% to 100% higher than stocks in other countries.

The dollar recycling system stems from the oil market, which is historically a major US import product. As the US buys goods and exports US dollars, foreign countries use those dollars to buy US bonds and stock investments. Thus, if the US wants to close its trade deficit, fewer US dollars will flow into foreign countries, meaning fewer will flow back into the US financial system.

More broadly, I suspect US bonds are declining simply due to a fall in global perception of stability. Although people's opinions on the stability of the US government vary greatly, I think it's clear that global perception has waned in light of extreme volatility and escalation regarding tariffs. Investors do not like uncertainty, and the US's value as a global haven is limited in light of rapid and significant changes in trade and geopolitical policy.

For me, the essential question is when the bond crash becomes a determinantal positive feedback loop. Currently, around 36% of tax revenue goes to interest payments. This is a more significant cost than the military budget or Medicare. It has risen sharply since 2022 due primarily to higher rates and secondarily to higher debt:

This figure has not risen this year primarily because of the US debt ceiling. The US reached its debt ceiling early this year, meaning it cannot continue to raise debt. Instead, it uses "extraordinary measures" (not investing in retirement funds, etc.) to maintain the fiscal budget without adding new debt. This still creates a liability that delays more significant Treasury auction sales later this year, assuming a new debt limit is agreed on. This must occur by the summer as the US is expected to default. After that, I expect a more significant spike in interest costs as new debt is sold at higher rates.

I believe the key factor will be if there soon becomes a global race out of US dollar-denominated debt. The reality is that this risk is not limited to Treasuries, and includes the ~30% of AGG's holdings that are not Treasuries or mortgage-backed securities. All US debt is at risk because the government is not likely to default on its debt, but instead create money through the Federal Reserve to meet obligations, likely spurring higher inflation. As US inflation rises, more investors will look to move out of US dollar-denominated debt, pushing the deficit higher, spurring more money printing, and more inflation.

Theoretically, this could cause inflation to rise to an extreme high. Ideally, this process could occur slowly, wherein inflation increases to 5-10% and remains for years until the US debt-to-GDP falls. However, if there is a concerted effort by foreign investors to limit exposure to US dollar bonds due to this, I suspect the process would be accelerated and potentially result in hyperinflation. If so, all US dollar bonds, including short-term bonds, may be devalued. Long-term bonds, particularly the 15-year+ maturity bonds, have the most significant risk.

The Federal Reserve is in a very awkward situation. It is mandated to mitigate inflation without causing recessions. However, if prices rise due to tariffs, its ability to use its tools to lower inflation is limited as doing so would likely exacerbate negative economic pressures.

This week, Trump petitioned the Supreme Court to have the ability to fire Federal regulators from independent agencies such as the Federal Reserve. Trump previously said he has no intention of removing Powell but is now pressuring the Fed to cut rates to ease the economic impact of tariffs. Of course, rate cuts would also likely exacerbate associated inflationary pressures and accelerate US dollar exchange rate declines, which would likely steepen the yield curve as foreign investors sell long-term bonds (due to inflation devaluation risk).

Indeed, we're starting to see a strong yield curve steepening, with 10-year rates now 56 bps higher than two-year:

Although a steep yield curve is seen as a positive factor, a steepening yield curve is associated with a recession. Almost all recessions since 1980 coincided with a yield curve steepening after a period of inversion. I suspect the steepening trend may be extreme, given that the curve has been inverted for an inordinately long period since 2022.

What is AGG's Downside Risk?

AGG is a lower-risk investment fund, but it is far riskier than its historical volatility level suggests. Its weighted average maturity is about 8.2 years, and its duration is about 5.8 years.

AGG's duration tells us that a 1% rise in its yield, with a YTM of 4.8% today, will push its price down by about 5.8%. To compare, the extreme long-duration bond ETF (ZROZ) has a duration of nearly 28 years, giving it extreme downside risk with a slight rise in its yield. The 1-3 year Treasury ETF (SHY) has a duration of just 1.8 years, giving it minor risk from an increase in rates, mitigated by its low maturity.

AGG downside risk is about a quarter of long maturity funds like ZROZ, (TLT), or (EDV), but around 4X higher than low maturity funds like SHY. That said, if my outlook of a spiraling inflationary crisis in bonds occurs within the coming years, its downside risk could be pretty significant, particularly if that results in hyperinflation.

In reality, that risk cannot be predicted because two men, Powell and Trump, arguably are the most significant driving factors. Powell is somewhat predictable, but for me, Trump is not. That said, given the trend in the US deficit, interest rates, and the recent reaction in the bond market to Trump's decisions, I think the odds of a hyperinflation crisis are rising.

I do not see how the US government can balance its budget within the necessary period without dramatically cutting Social Security, Medicaid, and Medicare, which would be politically untenable. The non-military, non-mandatory spending portion, used for government agencies being downsized by DOGE efforts, is only around 10% of government spending, so even an unlikely 50% cut in agency spending would not fix the deficit.

AGG Alternatives - Look Overseas

Overall, I am bearish on AGG. I do not expect it will sustain a rally if the stock market declines because this issue is largely driven by the risk that the US inflation rate will rise due to debt monetization, potentially triggered by an ongoing wave of foreign selling of US dollar bonds. This is bearish for all of AGG's holdings because all are dollar-denominated, but short-term bonds are at significantly lower risk.

In my view, the best alternatives to AGG are likely foreign bonds. The lower-risk international Treasury bond ETF (IGOV) may be suitable, given its in developed-country bonds that rise in value as the dollar declines. However, its yield is lower at about 2.8%. Another option is the Emerging Market local currency bond (EMLC), which is riskier, but will also rise with a weaker dollar and pays a higher yield of ~5.9%. Of course, short-term bond funds like (SHY) or T-Bills (BIL) have limited duration risk from the steepening yield curve. That said, I suspect short-term Treasury bonds may still face abnormal volatility if a global race out of US debt accelerates. More speculatively, the Swiss Franc ETF (FXF) is rising quickly, being the primary benefactor of the falling US dollar, positioning itself as the new global currency haven.

data.title: Unpacking Global Fiscal And Trade Imbalances
data.description:
Transcript

Ken Chambers: Global uncertainty, U.S. exceptionalism coming under question. And really this shift in priorities towards national interests. What were some of the discussion points?

Tiffany Wilding: Yeah. So clearly, we have a new potential disruptor.

And we would argue these various policy pivots, all kind of come back to the interconnected issue of global trade imbalances.

What the administration would argue is that efficient market mechanisms should effectively balance trade over time. So an example of that is if you have a country that has a competitive advantage in some product or service, they'll export, they have an initial trade surplus.

But as a result of that, they also are getting higher domestic incomes from all of those profits coming back into the country, and they consume more. And as they consume more, they import more things. You probably also have a currency adjustment and appreciation that's happening. And over time, that helps rebalance trade. So you effectively are exporting to import.

But if you look at the last 40 years of trade within the global economy, what you see is that mechanism clearly has not happened. So we have countries that have effectively run serial trade surpluses China, Japan, maybe before the pandemic, also places like Germany, Korea, Singapore against other countries in the global economy that run serial trade deficits, that's the United States. But also, you have countries like the UK and France that do as well.

So there's a key question around why is that happening, why are we not getting that rebalancing? And the administration would argue it's because of policies, regulatory, other sort of structural policies within these trade surplus countries that effectively depress consumption. They require their household sector to subsidize manufacturing. And so, the result of that has been places that don't do that, like the United States, they can't compete. And we've had a hollowing out of the middle class. We've had a reduction in manufacturing share in the United States because we can't compete with this. As a result of how the government's social safety net is set up, that's resulted in automatic stabilizer type of programs that have increased the US federal fiscal deficit. So things like Medicare, food assistance programs, US disability programs, all have had increased pickup as a result of what economists call this a China shock.

So, as you can see as I'm talking, these are structural issues that require structural adjustments that will be difficult. And I think that just puts in perspective the broader discussion that's happening in Washington around really cutting federal government spending and fiscal deficits.

So all of this is to say is that the fed has some tricky decisions that they're going to make. We think it really will boil down to our recession risks rising faster than inflation risks. And on this point, I would say that there has been recently a very uncomfortable increase in inflation expectations across the various consumer surveys and business surveys more recently, and this could eventually constrain the fed. It's obviously going in the direction they don't want it to go. We think what it means is the more preemptive cuts that the fed did in 2018, 2019, for example, against potential weakness, they could be more constrained in that. But nevertheless, if they're seeing the unemployment rate increase, we think they're cutting aggressively as those recession risks are increasing.

But the US is not the only place that needs to make these adjustments. Elsewhere, like China, other places also need to make those adjustments. The US is trying to force that through higher tariffs. That's creating some disruption. And it's really yet to be seen how much additional price level adjustment, how much additional market volatility the US voter base is willing to tolerate to get through this period. So I think all of this is to say, is that these structural adjustments, they have important constraints. And that's just creating a lot of near-term uncertainty.

Disclosure

The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed.
